title: Basic Two-Phase Locking Protocol
description: Implementation demonstrating 2PL with growing and shrinking phases
code:
PROCEDURE TwoPhaseLock(transaction_id, data_item, lock_type)
BEGIN
 IF lock_type = "SHARED" THEN
 IF data_item has X-lock THEN
 WAIT
 ELSE
 Grant S-lock on data_item to transaction_id
 Add lock to growing_locks[transaction_id]
 END IF
 ELSIF lock_type = "EXCLUSIVE" THEN
 IF data_item has S-lock OR X-lock THEN
 WAIT
 ELSE
 Grant X-lock on data_item to transaction_id
 Add lock to growing_locks[transaction_id]
 END IF
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ReleaseLocks(transaction_id)
BEGIN
 // Shrinking phase begins
 FOR EACH lock IN growing_locks[transaction_id] DO
 Release lock on data_item
 END FOR
 Clear growing_locks[transaction_id]
END PROCEDURE
explanation: This pseudocode shows how locks are acquired during growing phase and released during shrinking phase. Shared locks allow concurrent reads, while exclusive locks serialize write operations.

complexity.time: O(n) where n is number of locks
complexity.space: O(t) where t is number of transactions

title: Transaction Execution with ACID Properties
description: Pseudo-code showing transaction with commit and rollback handling
code:
PROCEDURE ExecuteTransaction(operations[])
BEGIN
 // Begin transaction
 transaction_id = BEGIN_TRANSACTION()
 
 TRY
 // Active state
 FOR EACH operation IN operations DO
 Acquire locks as needed (Isolation)
 Execute operation
 Update undo/redo log (Atomicity)
 END FOR
 
 // Partially committed
 Write COMMIT marker to log
 Force log to stable storage (Durability)
 Release locks
 
 // Committed state
 RETURN SUCCESS
 
 EXCEPT
 // Failed state
 Rollback using undo log (Atomicity)
 Release locks
 // Aborted state
 RETURN FAILURE
 END TRY
END PROCEDURE
explanation: Demonstrates how ACID properties are implemented: locks for isolation, undo logs for atomicity (rollback), redo logs for durability (commit), and constraint checks for consistency.

complexity.time: O(n log n) for n operations with logging

title: Deadlock Detection using Wait-For Graph
description: Algorithm to detect deadlocks in transaction processing
code:
PROCEDURE DetectDeadlock()
BEGIN
 Initialize empty graph
 
 FOR EACH active_transaction T1 IN transactions DO
 FOR EACH lock L1 held by T1 DO
 FOR EACH waiting_transaction T2 DO
 IF T2 is waiting for lock L1 THEN
 Add edge T2 → T1 to graph
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Detect cycles using DFS
 FOR EACH node IN graph DO
 IF HasCycleFrom(node) THEN
 RETURN DEADLOCK_DETECTED
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN NO_DEADLOCK
END PROCEDURE

PROCEDURE ResolveDeadlock()
BEGIN
 victim = SelectVictim() // based on youngest/lowest priority
 Rollback(victim)
 Restart(victim) OR Kill(victim)
END PROCEDURE
explanation: The wait-for graph tracks which transactions are waiting for locks held by others. A cycle indicates deadlock. The system resolves by selecting a victim transaction to abort and break the deadlock.

title: Precedence Graph for Serializability Testing
description: Algorithm to check if schedule is conflict serializable
code:
PROCEDURE CheckSerializability(schedule)
BEGIN
 Initialize empty adjacency list
 transactions = GetAllTransactions(schedule)
 
 FOR EACH pair of operations (op1, op2) IN schedule DO
 IF op1 and op2 conflict THEN
 // op1 must come before op2 in serial order
 t1 = GetTransaction(op1)
 t2 = GetTransaction(op2)
 IF op1 occurs_before op2 IN schedule THEN
 Add edge t1 → t2
 ELSE
 Add edge t2 → t1
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Check for cycles using DFS
 FOR EACH transaction IN transactions DO
 IF DetectCycleDFS(transaction, visited, recursion_stack) THEN
 RETURN NOT_SERIALIZABLE
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN SERIALIZABLE
END PROCEDURE
explanation: Constructs a precedence graph where edges represent conflicts between operations. If the graph has no cycles, the schedule is conflict serializable. Cycles indicate potential data consistency problems.
